sentences.base_language: Tomorrow I have to give an important presentation at work.
sentences.target_language: Demain je dois faire une présentation importante au travail.
sentences.grammarNote: Devoir + infinitive expresses obligation ('must/have to'): je dois travailler, tu dois partir. Its strength varies by context: je dois means 'I must/have to'; je devrais (conditional) softens it to 'I should'.

sentences.base_language: I'm very nervous. I barely slept last night.
sentences.target_language: Je suis très nerveux. J'ai à peine dormi cette nuit.
sentences.grammarNote:
**À peine** (barely / hardly) is a restrictive adverb placed after the auxiliary in compound tenses: *j'ai à peine dormi*, *il a à peine mangé*.
In literary French, *à peine* can trigger inversion at the start of a sentence: *à peine avait-il dormi…*
**Cette nuit** (last night / tonight) — in French, *cette nuit* can mean either last night or tonight depending on context. 'Last night' can also be *hier soir* (yesterday evening).

sentences.base_language: I keep thinking about everything that could go wrong.
sentences.target_language: Je n'arrête pas de penser à tout ce qui pourrait mal se passer.
sentences.grammarNote: Ne pas arrêter de + infinitive means 'to keep doing something / to not stop doing something': il n'arrête pas de parler, elle n'arrête pas de sourire. It expresses a repeated or continuous action.

sentences.base_language: My friend can see that I'm stressed.
sentences.target_language: Mon ami voit bien que je suis stressé.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Voir** (to see) is irregular: je vois, tu vois, il **voit**, nous voyons, vous voyez, ils voient.
**Voit bien que** (can clearly see that) — **bien** here acts as an intensifier meaning 'clearly / obviously': *tu vois bien que*, *on comprend bien que*.
**Que je suis stressé** — after *voir que* (to see that), the indicative is used, not the subjunctive, because the verb expresses perception of a fact.

sentences.base_language: Calm down. You have prepared so well.
sentences.target_language: Calme-toi. Tu as tellement bien préparé.
sentences.grammarNote: Calme-toi is the imperative of the reflexive verb se calmer. With reflexive verbs in the imperative, the reflexive pronoun follows the verb and is attached by a hyphen: lève-toi, assieds-toi, dépêche-toi.

sentences.base_language: I know, but what if I forget everything?
sentences.target_language: Je sais, mais et si j'oublie tout ?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Et si + present tense** expresses a hypothetical worry ('what if…?'): *et si j'oublie tout?*, *et si ça ne marche pas?*
This is different from a conditional clause (*si + imparfait*) — *et si* + present is an anxious rhetorical question, not a real if-then condition.
**Tout** (everything) is used as a pronoun here, meaning 'everything': *j'oublie tout*, *il sait tout*, *on a tout préparé*.

sentences.base_language: You won't forget. You know this topic perfectly.
sentences.target_language: Tu n'oublieras pas. Tu connais ce sujet parfaitement.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tu n'oublieras pas** — the **futur simple** is used here to reassure: *tu n'oublieras pas* (you will not forget).
Futur simple of *oublier*: j'oublierai, tu **oublieras**, il oubliera — formed by adding endings to the infinitive: -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont.
**Connaître vs. savoir** — both mean 'to know': *connaître* is for knowing people, places, and subjects familiarly (*tu connais ce sujet*); *savoir* is for knowing facts or how to do things (*tu sais faire ça*).

sentences.base_language: Take a deep breath. One thing at a time.
sentences.target_language: Prends une grande inspiration. Une chose à la fois.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Prends** is the **imperative** (tu) of *prendre* — the informal singular command: *prends*, *viens*, *fais*, *dis*.
Note that *-er* verbs lose the -s in the tu imperative (*parle!*, *mange!*), but irregular verbs like *prendre* keep it: *prends*.
**À la fois** (at a time / at once) is a fixed expression: *une chose à la fois* (one thing at a time), *tout à la fois* (everything at once), *à la fois A et B* (both A and B).

sentences.base_language: You're right. I'm going to be fine.
sentences.target_language: Tu as raison. Ça va aller.
sentences.grammarNote: Ça va aller is a reassuring phrase meaning 'it will be okay'. Aller can describe states and how things are going beyond just movement: comment ça va ? (how are you?), ça va bien (things are going well), ça va aller (it will work out).

sentences.base_language: Everyone in the room will be rooting for you.
sentences.target_language: Tout le monde dans la salle sera pour toi.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tout le monde** (everyone) is grammatically **singular** in French — it always takes a singular verb: *tout le monde **sera***, *tout le monde **est***, *tout le monde **pense***.
**Sera** is the **futur simple** of *être*: je serai, tu seras, il/elle **sera**, nous serons, vous serez, ils seront.
**Pour toi** — the stressed pronoun **toi** is used after prepositions: *pour toi*, *avec toi*, *sans toi*.

**Ça m'aide** — **ça** (that/it) is the informal spoken form of *cela*; it is the subject of the sentence.
**M'aide** — the indirect/direct object pronoun **me** contracts to **m'** before a vowel: *ça m'aide*, *ça m'intéresse*, *ça m'énerve*.
**Vraiment** (really) as an intensifying adverb follows the verb: *ça m'aide vraiment* (that really helps me). Compare: *ça **vraiment** m'aide* would be non-standard in spoken French.
